criteria.A: Persistent deficits in social communication and social interaction across settings.
criteria.B: Restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or sensory responses are present.
criteria.C: Symptoms begin early in development, impair daily functioning, and are not better explained by intellectual disability alone.

symptoms.text: reduced eye contact or nonverbal communication

name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder

criteria.A: A persistent pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity interferes with functioning or development.
criteria.B: Several symptoms are present before age 12 and occur in two or more settings such as home, school, or work.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause clear impairment and are not better explained by another mental disorder.

duration: Chronic pattern with onset before age 12

criteria.A: Persistent difficulties in learning and using language across speaking, writing, sign language, or understanding.
criteria.B: Language ability is clearly below what is expected for age and causes functional limits in communication, school, or work.
criteria.C: Symptoms begin in development and are not better explained by hearing loss, intellectual disability, or another condition.

symptoms.text: difficulty telling stories or explaining ideas

criteria.A: Learning and execution of coordinated motor skills are substantially below age expectations.
criteria.B: Motor problems interfere with self-care, school tasks, play, or productivity.
criteria.C: Symptoms begin early in development and are not due to intellectual disability, visual impairment, or a neurological disease.

symptoms.text: slow handwriting or fine motor work
difficulty learning sports or coordinated tasks

criteria.A: Multiple motor tics and at least one vocal tic have been present at some point, though not always at the same time.
criteria.B: Tics wax and wane but persist for more than 1 year since first tic onset.
criteria.C: Onset occurs before age 18 and symptoms are not due to a substance or another medical condition.

category: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders
name: Delusional Disorder

criteria.A: One or more delusions are present for at least 1 month.
criteria.B: Schizophrenia criteria have never been fully met, and functioning outside the delusion is not markedly odd.
criteria.C: Mood episodes are brief compared with the delusional periods, and symptoms are not due to substances or medical illness.

category: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders
name: Brief Psychotic Disorder

criteria.A: Sudden onset of one or more psychotic symptoms such as delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, or grossly disorganized behavior.
criteria.B: At least one symptom is delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized speech.
criteria.C: Episode lasts at least 1 day but less than 1 month, with eventual full return to previous functioning, and is not better explained by mood disorder or substances.

category: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders
name: Schizophreniform Disorder

criteria.A: Two or more core psychotic symptoms are present, with at least one being delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized speech.
criteria.B: Symptoms last long enough to meet the syndrome but the total duration is less than 6 months.
criteria.C: Mood and medical causes do not better explain the presentation.

category: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders
name: Schizophrenia

criteria.A: Two or more core psychotic symptoms are present for a significant portion of time, and at least one is delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized speech.
criteria.B: Functioning in work, relationships, or self-care is markedly worse than before the illness.
criteria.C: Continuous signs of disturbance persist for at least 6 months and are not better explained by mood disorder, substances, or medical illness.

category: Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders
name: Schizoaffective Disorder

criteria.A: An uninterrupted illness includes a major mood episode together with core schizophrenia symptoms.
criteria.B: There is a period of at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations without a major mood episode.
criteria.C: Mood symptoms are present for most of the total illness duration, and the condition is not due to substances or medical illness.

duration: Ongoing illness with at least 2 weeks of psychosis without mood episode

name: Bipolar I Disorder

criteria.A: At least one manic episode has occurred, marked by abnormally elevated or irritable mood and increased energy.
criteria.B: During the manic episode, symptoms such as decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, racing thoughts, pressured speech, or risky behavior are clearly present.
criteria.C: Mood disturbance causes major impairment, may include psychosis or hospitalization, and is not due to substances or medical illness.

duration: Mania lasts at least 1 week or any duration if hospitalized

name: Bipolar II Disorder

criteria.A: There has been at least one hypomanic episode and at least one major depressive episode.
criteria.B: There has never been a full manic episode.
criteria.C: Mood episodes cause clinically significant distress or impairment and are not better explained by psychotic disorders.

duration: Hypomania lasts at least 4 days; depression lasts at least 2 weeks

criteria.A: For at least 2 years, numerous periods of hypomanic symptoms and depressive symptoms have occurred without meeting full episode criteria.
criteria.B: Symptoms are present for at least half the time, and the person has not been symptom-free for more than 2 months at a time.
criteria.C: The pattern causes distress or impairment and is not better explained by bipolar disorder, substances, or medical illness.

criteria.A: Severe recurrent temper outbursts are grossly out of proportion to the situation and inconsistent with developmental level.
criteria.B: Between outbursts, mood is persistently irritable or angry most of the day, nearly every day, in multiple settings.
criteria.C: Symptoms last at least 12 months, begin before age 10, and are not better explained by bipolar disorder or another condition.

symptoms.text: problems at home school or with peers

criteria.A: Five or more depressive symptoms are present during the same 2-week period, including depressed mood or loss of interest.
criteria.B: Symptoms cause distress or impairment and may include sleep, appetite, energy, guilt, concentration, or suicidal changes.
criteria.C: Episode is not due to substances or medical illness and is not better explained by bipolar disorder.

criteria.A: Depressed mood is present most of the day, more days than not, for a long period.
criteria.B: During the depressed mood, at least two associated symptoms such as poor appetite, insomnia, low energy, low self-esteem, poor concentration, or hopelessness are present.
criteria.C: Symptoms persist for the required duration without long symptom-free gaps and are not better explained by bipolar disorder or another condition.

criteria.A: In most menstrual cycles, mood symptoms appear in the final week before menses and improve shortly after onset of menses.
criteria.B: Symptoms include marked mood swings, irritability, depressed mood, or anxiety plus additional physical or behavioral symptoms.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause distress or impairment and are not simply an exacerbation of another disorder.

name: Separation Anxiety Disorder

criteria.A: Developmentally inappropriate fear or anxiety about separation from attachment figures is present.
criteria.B: Symptoms may include distress when separated, worry about harm, refusal to go out alone, nightmares, or physical complaints.
criteria.C: Fear lasts long enough to be clinically significant and is not better explained by another disorder.

symptoms.text: worry that loved ones will be harmed

duration: At least 4 weeks in children or 6 months in adults
keywords: fear of being away from parents

criteria.A: Marked fear or anxiety occurs about a specific object or situation.
criteria.B: The feared object or situation almost always triggers immediate anxiety and is actively avoided or endured with intense fear.
criteria.C: Fear is out of proportion, lasts at least 6 months, causes impairment, and is not better explained by another disorder.

symptoms.text: intense fear of a specific trigger
fear feels excessive but hard to control

criteria.A: Marked fear or anxiety about social situations involving possible scrutiny by others.
criteria.B: The person fears acting in a way that will be negatively judged, and social situations are avoided or endured with intense anxiety.
criteria.C: Fear is persistent, out of proportion, causes impairment, and is not better explained by another disorder or medical condition.

symptoms.text: blushing or shaking in social settings

name: Panic Disorder

criteria.A: Recurrent unexpected panic attacks occur with sudden surges of intense fear and physical symptoms.
criteria.B: At least one attack is followed by persistent worry about more attacks or maladaptive behavior change related to the attacks.
criteria.C: Symptoms are not due to substances, medical illness, or another mental disorder.

symptoms.text: fear of dying or losing control

duration: At least 1 month of worry or behavior change after attacks

criteria.A: Marked fear or anxiety occurs about situations where escape might be difficult or help may not be available if panic-like symptoms happen.
criteria.B: Situations are actively avoided, require a companion, or are endured with intense fear.
criteria.C: Fear is persistent, causes impairment, and is not better explained by another disorder.

symptoms.text: avoidance of places that feel hard to escape

criteria.A: Excessive anxiety and worry occur more days than not about multiple events or activities.
criteria.B: The worry is hard to control and is associated with symptoms such as restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, or sleep problems.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause distress or impairment and are not due to substances, medical illness, or another mental disorder.

name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

criteria.A: Obsessions, compulsions, or both are present.
criteria.B: Obsessions are intrusive unwanted thoughts, and compulsions are repetitive behaviors or mental acts aimed at reducing distress or preventing feared outcomes.
criteria.C: Symptoms are time-consuming or impairing and are not due to substances or another disorder.

duration: Usually persistent; often more than 1 hour per day

criteria.A: There is a preoccupation with one or more perceived defects in appearance that are not observable or appear slight to others.
criteria.B: The person performs repetitive behaviors or mental acts in response to appearance concerns.
criteria.C: Preoccupation causes distress or impairment and is not better explained by an eating disorder.

symptoms.text: avoiding social situations because of looks

criteria.A: Persistent difficulty discarding possessions occurs regardless of actual value.
criteria.B: The difficulty is driven by a perceived need to save items and distress linked to discarding them.
criteria.C: Accumulation congests living areas or causes significant distress or impairment and is not better explained by another condition.

criteria.A: Recurrent pulling out of one's hair leads to hair loss.
criteria.B: The person repeatedly tries to stop or reduce the behavior.
criteria.C: The behavior causes distress or impairment and is not better explained by another medical or mental condition.

criteria.A: Recurrent skin picking leads to skin lesions.
criteria.B: The person repeatedly tries to stop or reduce the picking.
criteria.C: The behavior causes distress or impairment and is not better explained by another condition.

name: Reactive Attachment Disorder

criteria.A: A child shows a consistent pattern of emotionally withdrawn behavior toward adult caregivers.
criteria.B: There is persistent social and emotional disturbance such as minimal comfort-seeking, limited positive affect, or unexplained irritability or fearfulness.
criteria.C: The pattern follows severe neglect or insufficient caregiving, begins before age 5, and is not better explained by autism spectrum disorder.

duration: Begins before age 5 after severe neglect

criteria.A: Exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence is followed by intrusive trauma symptoms.
criteria.B: There is persistent avoidance plus negative changes in mood or thinking and marked arousal or reactivity.
criteria.C: Symptoms last more than 1 month, cause impairment, and are not due to substances or medical illness.

criteria.A: After trauma exposure, the person develops intrusive, negative mood, dissociative, avoidance, or arousal symptoms.
criteria.B: At least nine symptoms from these clusters are present.
criteria.C: Symptoms begin or worsen after the trauma, last from 3 days to 1 month, and cause impairment.

name: Adjustment Disorder

criteria.A: Emotional or behavioral symptoms develop in response to an identifiable stressor.
criteria.B: Distress is out of proportion to the stressor and/or causes meaningful impairment in functioning.
criteria.C: Symptoms begin within 3 months of the stressor, do not represent normal bereavement alone, and do not persist long after the stressor ends.

symptoms.text: trouble coping after a life change

duration: Begins within 3 months of stressor; usually resolves within 6 months after stressor ends

criteria.A: There is disruption of identity with two or more distinct personality states or an experience of possession.
criteria.B: The disruption includes recurrent gaps in memory for everyday events, important information, or traumatic events.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause distress or impairment and are not a normal cultural practice or due to substances or medical illness.

criteria.A: There is an inability to recall important autobiographical information, usually related to trauma or stress.
criteria.B: The memory problem is too extensive to be ordinary forgetting.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause distress or impairment and are not due to substances, neurological illness, or another disorder.

symptoms.text: memory gaps after stress or trauma

criteria.A: Persistent or recurrent experiences of depersonalization, derealization, or both are present.
criteria.B: Reality testing remains intact during the episodes.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause distress or impairment and are not due to substances, medical illness, or another mental disorder.

criteria.A: One or more distressing physical symptoms are present.
criteria.B: There are excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviors related to the symptoms, such as persistent worry, high health anxiety, or excessive time and energy devoted to them.
criteria.C: The symptomatic state is persistent even if a specific medical explanation is absent or changes.

symptoms.text: a lot of time focused on bodily symptoms

criteria.A: There is a preoccupation with having or getting a serious illness.
criteria.B: Somatic symptoms are absent or mild, but anxiety about health is high, and the person easily becomes alarmed about bodily sensations.
criteria.C: The illness fear is persistent, causes impairment, and is not better explained by another mental disorder.

symptoms.text: avoiding doctors or repeatedly seeing doctors

criteria.A: One or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function are present.
criteria.B: Clinical findings show incompatibility between the symptom and recognized neurological or medical conditions.
criteria.C: Symptoms cause distress or impairment or warrant medical evaluation and are not better explained by another disorder.

symptoms.text: swallowing difficulty without clear neurological cause

name: Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder

criteria.A: An eating disturbance leads to persistent failure to meet nutritional or energy needs.
criteria.B: The disturbance causes weight loss, nutritional deficiency, dependence on supplements or tube feeding, or marked interference with psychosocial functioning.
criteria.C: It is not driven by body-image concerns and is not better explained by lack of food, a medical condition, or another mental disorder alone.

symptoms.text: fear of eating because of texture or choking

keywords: poor nutrition without body image concerns

criteria.A: Restriction of energy intake leads to significantly low body weight.
criteria.B: There is intense fear of gaining weight or persistent behavior that interferes with weight gain.
criteria.C: Self-evaluation is overly influenced by body weight or shape, or the seriousness of low weight is not recognized.

symptoms.text: denial of seriousness of low weight

name: Bulimia Nervosa

criteria.A: Recurrent binge eating occurs with a sense of loss of control.
criteria.B: Recurrent compensatory behaviors are used to prevent weight gain, such as vomiting, laxatives, fasting, or excessive exercise.
criteria.C: The binge-compensatory pattern occurs regularly, self-worth is overly tied to body shape or weight, and it does not occur only during anorexia nervosa.

duration: At least once a week for 3 months

name: Binge-Eating Disorder

criteria.A: Recurrent binge eating episodes occur with a sense of loss of control.
criteria.B: Binges are associated with features such as eating very rapidly, eating when not hungry, eating until uncomfortably full, eating alone because of embarrassment, or feeling guilty afterward.
criteria.C: Episodes cause marked distress, occur regularly, and are not accompanied by recurrent compensatory behaviors.

duration: At least once a week for 3 months

criteria.A: A problematic pattern of alcohol use leads to impairment or distress, shown by multiple control, social, risky-use, or physical dependence symptoms.
criteria.B: The person may drink more than intended, fail to cut down, spend a lot of time using alcohol, crave it, or keep drinking despite harm.
criteria.C: Severity depends on the number of symptoms present within a 12-month period.

symptoms.text: continues despite relationship or work problems

criteria.A: A problematic pattern of cannabis use leads to impairment or distress with multiple substance-use symptoms.
criteria.B: The person may use more than intended, fail to cut down, crave cannabis, or continue despite social, work, or health problems.
criteria.C: Severity depends on the number of symptoms within a 12-month period.

criteria.A: A problematic pattern of opioid use leads to impairment or distress with multiple substance-use symptoms.
criteria.B: The person may use more than intended, fail to cut down, crave opioids, or continue despite harm, and may develop tolerance or withdrawal.
criteria.C: Severity depends on symptom count within a 12-month period.

criteria.A: A problematic pattern of stimulant use leads to impairment or distress with multiple substance-use symptoms.
criteria.B: The person may use more than intended, fail to cut down, crave stimulants, or continue despite social, occupational, or medical harm.
criteria.C: Severity depends on symptom count within a 12-month period.

symptoms.text: withdrawal with fatigue or low mood

criteria.A: A problematic pattern of tobacco use leads to impairment or distress with multiple dependence symptoms.
criteria.B: The person may use more than intended, fail to quit, crave nicotine, or continue despite health problems.
criteria.C: Severity depends on symptom count within a 12-month period.

criteria.A: A pattern of angry or irritable mood, argumentative or defiant behavior, or vindictiveness is present.
criteria.B: Symptoms occur during interaction with at least one person who is not a sibling and cause impairment.
criteria.C: The pattern is not better explained by psychotic, substance, depressive, or bipolar disorders.

name: Antisocial Personality Disorder

criteria.A: There is a pervasive pattern of disregard for and violation of the rights of others since age 15.
criteria.B: The pattern includes behaviors such as deceitfulness, impulsivity, aggression, irresponsibility, or lack of remorse, and the person is at least age 18 with evidence of conduct disorder before age 15.
criteria.C: Behavior is not exclusively during schizophrenia or bipolar disorder.

duration: Stable pattern since adolescence or early adulthood

name: Borderline Personality Disorder

criteria.A: A pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, and emotions with marked impulsivity is present.
criteria.B: The pattern includes symptoms such as frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, self-harm, affective instability, emptiness, anger, or transient paranoia/dissociation.
criteria.C: The pattern is stable across time and situations and begins by early adulthood.

duration: Long-standing pattern beginning by early adulthood
